最新的表现是《纽约时报》的一篇报道，总统正在任命阴谋理论家西德尼·鲍威尔（Sidney Powell）担任特别顾问，该阴谋理论家曾在其法律团队中工作过，他将专门调查假想的选民欺诈指控。
  似乎还不够，我们还了解到，前国家安全顾问迈克尔·弗林在承认对联邦调查局撒谎后被总统赦免，他参加了星期五的会议。在本周早些时候，已退休的中将弗林（Flynn）提出了一个想法（他以前曾提倡过），即总统实施戒严令并部署军队在几个投票反对特朗普的竞争激烈的州“重新进行”选举。似乎弗林想把他们变成真正的战场状态。
  这些都不应该让人感到意外。我们中有些人说，即使在成为总统之前，唐纳德特朗普的罗塞塔石碑，关键要破译他的，是他的心理，他那一头凌乱的个性，他的情绪和精神状态不稳定，和他的反社会倾向。这是主要原因，尽管不是唯一原因，但尽管我曾在前三届共和党政府任职，但我拒绝在2016年或2020年投票支持他。特朗普在过去四年中所做的任何事情都没有使我重新考虑我的评估，并且发生了很多事情来证实这一点。
  考虑到特朗普的心理状况，不可避免的是，当他感觉到现实的墙在他身边的时候— 2020年，是大流行，经济崩溃和他的大选失败，他将使自己与现实进一步分离。可以预见，总统会断言更奇怪的阴谋论。他会变得更加愤怒和沮丧，更加绝望和沮丧，更加被他的委屈所消耗。他将与总检察长比尔·巴尔和佐治亚州州长布莱恩·坎普等过往的恳求者抗衡，并对他的敌人更加激进。用李尔王的话说，他的智慧将开始转向。他将开始失去理智。
  所以他有。结果，特朗普总统变得更加不稳定和危险。
 “我一直在报道唐纳德·特朗普，” Axios的乔纳森·斯旺（Jonathan Swan）发推文说。 “我不记得听到实际上是特朗普人民的高级官员的强烈关注。悉尼·鲍威尔（Sidney Powell）/迈克尔·弗林（Michael Flynn）的想法正在顶部吸引了热情的听众。”
  即使在混乱中，也值得退后一步来思考我们的位置：一位美国总统不愿以700万张民意票和74张选举团的票数认输，仍在试图窃取选举。这已成为他的痴迷。 
在此过程中，特朗普在太多情况下将其政党变成了自由主义和虚无主义的工具。以下是要强调的几个数据点：18位司法部长和众议院一半以上的共和党人支持煽动滥用司法程序。
 这不仅是民选官员，甚至主要是民选官员。共和党的基地之后往往特朗普进入模糊地带，具有相当大的多数的他们肯定了拜登赢得大选基于欺诈行为，其中许多人在涌动流行的脸转向对医学科学。
 特朗普最亲密的顾问之一告诉《华盛顿邮报》，消息人士称，COVID-19目前正在以每分钟约一分钟的速度杀死美国人，但总统“只是用COVID做完了”。 “我认为他把它放在了时间表上，他已经完成了COVID的工作……这刚刚超过了他给它的时间。”
  在这里，特朗普严重的心理状况（他完全无法面对令人不快的事实，他的自恋和完全缺乏同情心）变得致命。特朗普的疏忽将原本艰难的冬天变成了一个黑暗的冬天。如果他的任何前任—巴拉克·奥巴马，乔治·W·布什，比尔·克林顿，乔治·HW·布什和罗纳德·里根，仅回溯到40岁，就曾在这一大流行时期担任过总统，那么几乎肯定会有成千上万的美国人过着这种生活已保存。
 马里兰州州长拉里·霍根说：“我担心的是，在战斗的最糟糕的时刻，将军在行动中失踪了。”他是特朗普时代讲真相的极少数共和党人之一。
  30天后，唐纳德·特朗普（Donald Trump）将离开总统府，而他发动政变的努力失败了。令人鼓舞的消息是，它从未真正有过成功的机会。我们的机构，特别是法院，将通过压力测试，这不是有史以来最困难的事情，但是足够困难，而且与我们历史上的任何经历都不一样。一些地方官员表现出勇气，在面对党派的威胁和压力时做正确的事情。在过去的四年中生活的美国公众中，有很多人应该取消这次总统怪胎秀而不是更新它，这是值得赞扬的。即使在大流行中，“疲惫的多数”也不是很累，无法出去投票。
  但是特朗普总统任期将在几乎所有地方留下巨大的伤口，并在某些地方造成破坏。真相作为一个概念已几乎每小时都在该国最高职位上遭受重创。共和党已经激化，无数共和党议员和党内其他知名人士甚至在特朗普被击败后，甚至在某些方面尤其显示出自己是道德moral夫。在特朗普担任总统期间，他们非常害怕与他和他的支持者相处，以致未能通过Solzhenitsyn测验：“一个普通勇敢的人的简单举动就是不撒谎，不支持虚假的举动！他的法则：让那东西进入世界，甚至让它统治至高无上-只是不要通过我。 
在过去的四年中，右翼生态系统变得越来越狂热。总统的许多福音派拥护者要么像富兰克林·格雷厄姆那样过时，要么像埃里克·梅塔克萨斯那样妄想，现在他们兜售自己的妄想是上帝所写的。 QAnon和《骄傲男孩》，Newsmax和《美国新闻》，Alex Jones和Tucker Carlson都胆大包天。
  这些令人担忧的趋势始于特朗普竞选公职之前，而这些趋势在他离开总统后不会消失。那些希望快速回弹的人会感到失望。尽管如此，让特朗普卸任还是有帮助的。他将发现没有可比的恶霸讲台。并且，如果媒体明智的话，它将切断他的氧气，这是引起注意的。他们别无选择，只能掩盖特朗普当总统时的挑衅行为。当他是前总统时，情况将会改变。
 在可预见的未来，记者将正确地关注流行病。但是，一旦遏制和制止了这一问题，就该回到将更多注意力集中在《巴黎协定》和碳税等问题上了。所得所得税抵免和基础设施；权利改革和货币政策；特许学校和校园语音守则；合法移民，庇护，同化和社会流动。与特朗普担任前总统一样，共和党还有机会再次成为理智的保守主义的家园。这是否发生是一个悬而未决的问题。但这是我们许多人愿意为之奋斗的东西，即使是进步主义者也应该希望。
  除此之外，更重要的是，我们必须提醒自己，塑造未来并非无能为力。大部分被破坏的东西可以修复；尽管我们很多，但我们可以合而为一；宿命论和犬儒主义是毫无根据和腐蚀的。
 威廉·华兹华斯（William Wordsworth）的诗《前奏》中有一句可爱的话：“我们所爱，别人会爱，我们将教他们如何。”
 仍有值得我们爱戴的事情。荣誉，体面，勇气，美丽和真理。温柔，人情和责任感。良好的社会。并致力于人类的尊严。我们需要在个人关系，教室和社区，读书俱乐部和圣经研究以及无数其他环境中教别人，为什么这些事值得他们注意，忠诚和爱戴。一个人这样做不会有多大改变；很多人这样做会创造一种文化。
 也许我们更好地理解比我们五年前为什么这些事情都是我们的生命来说是必不可少的，为什么当我们忽视他们或选举产生领导人谁嘲笑和颠覆他们的生活变得肮脏，野蛮，一般不愉快。 
1月20日正午之后，美国故事将开始一个新的必要章节。 乔·拜登（Joe Biden）当然会在塑造这个故事的结果中发挥作用-但是您和我也一样。如果我们能够保持下去，我们的共和国将是一个良好且可估计的共和国。
note_en:
The latest manifestation of this is  a report in  The New York Times that the president is weighing appointing  the conspiracy theorist Sidney Powell, who for a time worked on his legal team, to be special counsel to investigate imaginary claims of voter fraud.
  As if that were not enough, we also learned that former National Security Adviser Michael Flynn, who was pardoned by the president after pleading guilty to lying to the FBI, attended the Friday meeting. Earlier in the week, Flynn, a retired lieutenant general, floated the idea (which he had  promoted before) that the president impose martial law and deploy the military to “rerun” the election in several closely contested states that voted against Trump. It appears that Flynn wants to turn them into literal battleground states.
  None of this should come as a surprise. Some of us  said, even before he became president, that Donald Trump’s Rosetta Stone, the key to deciphering him, was his psychology—his disordered personality, his emotional and mental instability, and his sociopathic tendencies. It was the main reason, though hardly the only reason, I refused to vote for him in 2016 or in 2020, despite having worked in the three previous Republican administrations. Nothing that Trump has done over the past four years has caused me to rethink my assessment, and a great deal has happened to confirm it.
  Given Trump’s psychological profile, it was inevitable that when he felt the walls of reality close in on him—in 2020, it was the pandemic, the cratering economy, and his election defeat—he would detach himself even further from reality. It was predictable that the president would assert even more bizarre conspiracy theories. That he would become more enraged and embittered, more desperate and despondent, more consumed by his grievances. That he would go against past supplicants, like Attorney General Bill Barr and Georgia Governor Brian Kemp, and become more aggressive toward his perceived enemies. That his wits would begin to turn, in the words of King Lear. That he would begin to lose his mind.
  So he has. And, as a result, President Trump has become even more destabilizing and dangerous.
 “I’ve been covering Donald Trump for a while,” Jonathan Swan of  Axios  tweeted. “I can’t recall hearing more intense concern from senior officials who are actually Trump people. The Sidney Powell/Michael Flynn ideas are finding an enthusiastic audience at the top.”
  Even amid the chaos, it’s worth taking a step back to think about where we are: An American president, unwilling to concede his defeat by 7 million popular votes and 74 Electoral College votes, is  still trying to steal the election. It has become his obsession.
 In the process, Trump has in too many cases turned his party into an instrument of illiberalism and nihilism. Here are just a couple of data points to underscore that claim: 18 attorneys generals and more than half the Republicans in the House supported  a seditious abuse of the judicial process.
 And it’s not only, or even mainly, elected officials. The Republican Party’s base has often followed Trump into the twilight zone, with a sizable majority of them affirming that Joe Biden won the election based on fraud and many of them turning against medical science in the face of a surging pandemic.
 COVID-19 is now killing Americans at the rate of about one per minute, but the president is “just done with COVID,” a source identified as one of Trump’s closest advisers  told   The Washington Post. “I think he put it on a timetable and he’s done with COVID ... It just exceeded the amount of time he gave it.”
  This is where Trump’s crippling psychological condition—his complete inability to face unpleasant facts, his toxic narcissism, and his utter lack of empathy—became lethal. Trump’s negligence turned what would have been a difficult winter into a dark one. If any of his predecessors—Barack Obama, George W. Bush, Bill Clinton, George H. W. Bush, and Ronald Reagan, to go back just 40 years—had been president during this pandemic, tens of thousands of American lives would almost surely have been saved.
 “My concern was, in the worst part of the battle, the general was missing in action,”  said Maryland Governor Larry Hogan, one of the very few Republicans to speak truth in the Trump era.
  In 30 days, Donald Trump will leave the presidency, with his efforts to mount a coup having failed. The encouraging news is that it never really had a chance of succeeding. Our institutions, especially the courts, will have passed a stress test, not the most difficult ever but difficult enough, and unlike any in our history. Some local officials exhibited profiles in courage, doing the right thing in the face of threats and pressure from their party. And a preponderance of the American public, having lived through the past four years, deserve credit for canceling this presidential freak show rather than renewing it. The “ exhausted majority” wasn’t too exhausted to get out and vote, even in a pandemic.
  But the Trump presidency will leave gaping wounds nearly everywhere, and ruination in some places. Truth as a concept has been battered from the highest office in the land on an almost hourly basis. The Republican Party has been radicalized, with countless Republican lawmakers and other prominent figures within the party having revealed themselves to be moral cowards, even, and in some ways especially,  after Trump was defeated. During the Trump presidency, they were so afraid of getting crosswise with him and his supporters that they failed the  Solzhenitsyn test: “The simple act of an ordinary brave man is not to participate in lies, not to support false actions! His rule: Let  that come into the world, let it even reign supreme—only not through me.”
 During the past four years, the right-wing ecosystem became more and more rabid. Many prominent evangelical supporters of the president are either obsequious, like  Franklin Graham, or delusional, like  Eric Metaxas, and they now peddle their delusions as being written by God. QAnon and the Proud Boys,  Newsmax and One America News, Alex Jones and Tucker Carlson—all have been emboldened.
  These worrisome trends began before Trump ran for office, and they won’t disappear after he leaves the presidency. Those who hope for a quick snapback will be disappointed. Still, having Trump out of office has to help. He’s going to find out that there’s no comparable bully pulpit. And the media, if they are wise, will cut off his oxygen, which is attention. They had no choice but to cover Trump’s provocations when he was president; when he’s an ex-president, that will change.
 For the foreseeable future, journalists will rightly focus on the pandemic. But once that is contained and defeated, it will be time to go back to focusing more attention on things like the Paris Accords and the carbon tax; the earned-income tax credit and infrastructure; entitlement reform and monetary policy; charter schools and campus speech codes; legal immigration, asylum, assimilation, and social mobility. There is also an opportunity, with Trump a former president, for the Republican Party to once again become the home of sane conservatism. Whether that happens or not is an open question. But it’s something many of us are willing to work for, and that even progressives should hope for.
  Beyond that, and more fundamental than that, we have to remind ourselves that we are not powerless to shape the future; that much of what has been broken can be repaired; that though we are many, we can be one; and that fatalism and cynicism are unwarranted and corrosive.
 There’s a lovely line in William Wordsworth’s poem “ The Prelude”: “What we have loved, Others will love, and we will teach them how.”
 There are still things worthy of our love. Honor, decency, courage, beauty, and truth. Tenderness, human empathy, and a sense of duty. A good society. And a commitment to human dignity. We need to teach others—in our individual relationships, in our classrooms and communities, in our book clubs and Bible studies, and in innumerable other settings—why those things are worthy of their attention, their loyalty, their love. One person doing it won’t make much of a difference; a lot of people doing it will create a culture.
 Maybe we understand better than we did five years ago why these things are essential to our lives, and why when we neglect them or elect leaders who ridicule and subvert them, life becomes nasty, brutish, and generally unpleasant.
 Just after noon on January 20, a new and necessary chapter will begin in the American story. Joe Biden will certainly play a role in shaping how that story turns out—but so will you and I. Ours is a good and estimable republic, if we can keep it.

note:
上任后一月，当选总统乔·拜登将有很多责任。结束大流行，解决经济衰退，重建国家。即使这样，我还是提出了一个谦卑的请求，请他注意。拜登先生，我求你：废除太空部队。
  星期五，太空部队的灭亡是有史以来最强的理由。在一系列推文中，它向世界揭示了一个新的座右铭和名称。女士们，先生们，我提出：
  今天，经过为期一年的过程，涉及航天专业人员和广大公众的数百篇论文和研究成果，我们终于可以与您分享我们的名字：监护人。 pic.twitter.com/Tmlff4LKW6
—美国太空部队（@SpaceForceDoD）2020年12月18日
  遗产！任务！文化！你想了解更多吗？不幸的是，我会的。头脑只是在比赛。我从这则推文中推断出，我们创建了整个军事部门，以保护西方文明免受批判种族理论的侵害。这是太空同盟吗？
  命名部队的机会是一项重大责任。 Guardians是在太空作战中具有悠久历史的名字，其源头可以追溯到1983年美国空军太空司令部的原始格言“高级边防卫士”。
—美国太空部队（@SpaceForceDoD）2020年12月18日 
las，此推文未回答其前任启发的任何问题。我不想知道为什么他们被称为监护人。我想知道更多，例如谁负责这项决定。我们已经有国民警卫队和海岸警卫队；没有人能想到原创的东西？也许“突击队员”太明显了，不仅仅是因为我们在谈论太空中的部队。座右铭邀请进行某些历史比较。
  Guardians这个名字将我们引以为傲的遗产和文化与我们执行24/7的重要使命联系在一起，以保护美国及其盟国的人民和利益。
—美国太空部队（@SpaceForceDoD）2020年12月18日
  但是，我仍然想了解更多。这些制服会是什么样？排名如何工作？其余军人是否会认真对待太空部队？而且由于太空部队是一个全新的分支机构，“文化”和“遗产”可能指的是什么？他们没有！没有一个美国监护人（我灭亡！）除了在桌子上推铅笔外，什么也做不了。因为没有监护人。如果即将来临的白宫听我的话，也不会。
  我希望外星人是真实的，也希望他们正在前往这个星球。绑架我，这样做，使我不再想着太空部队。这是全世界最笨的狗屎。这将是整个银河系中最笨的狗屎。

note_en:
Upon taking office in January, President-elect Joe Biden will have many responsibilities. End the pandemic, solve the recession, rebuild the country. Even so, I submit a humble request for his attention. Mr. Biden, I beg you: Abolish the Space Force.
  On Friday, the Space Force made its strongest  case yet for its demise. In a series of tweets, it revealed a new motto and name to the world. Ladies and gentlemen, I present:
  Today, after a yearlong process that produced hundreds of submissions and research involving space professionals and members of the general public, we can finally share with you the name by which we will be known: Guardians.  pic.twitter.com/Tmlff4LKW6
— United States Space Force (@SpaceForceDoD)  December 18, 2020
  Heritage! Mission! Culture! Would you like to  know more? Unfortunately, I would. The mind simply races. I infer from this tweet that we created an entire branch of the military to guard Western civilization from critical race theory. Is this the Space Confederacy?
  The opportunity to name a force is a momentous responsibility. Guardians is a name with a long history in space operations, tracing back to the original command motto of Air Force Space Command in 1983, “Guardians of the High Frontier.”
— United States Space Force (@SpaceForceDoD)  December 18, 2020
  Alas, this tweet does not answer any of the questions its predecessor inspired. I don’t want to know why they’re called the Guardians. I would like to know more, such as who was in charge of this decision. We already have National Guardsmen, and Coast Guardsmen; nobody could think of something original? Maybe “stormtrooper” was too obvious, and not just because we’re talking about troops in space. The motto invites certain historical  comparisons.
  The name Guardians connects our proud heritage and culture to the important mission we execute 24/7, protecting the people and interest of the U.S. and its allies.
— United States Space Force (@SpaceForceDoD)  December 18, 2020
  But still, I would like to know more. What will those uniforms look like? How will rank work? Will the rest of the military ever take the Space Force seriously? And since the Space Force is a brand-new branch, to what could “culture” and “heritage” possibly refer? They have none! There isn’t a single U.S. Guardian (I perish!) who has done anything but push a pencil around a desk. Because there aren’t any Guardians. Nor will there be, if the incoming White House  listens to me.
  I hope that  aliens are real, and I hope they’re on their way to this planet. Abduct me and in so doing, spare me from thinking about the Space Force ever again. This is the dumbest shit in the entire world. It would be the dumbest shit in the entire galaxy.

 今天的问题是关于本周宣布的针对Google和Facebook的各种反托拉斯案件，以及为什么是时候对负责不良行为的高管提出刑事起诉。加：
  首先，一些房间整理。作家和精英的牛fly Anand Giridharadas就Facebook案采访了我。同样，我和反托拉斯律师Shaoul Sussman撰写了一篇关于中间派学者和历史学家Herb Hovenkamp的文章。 Hovenkamp在反托拉斯酒吧以外并不知名，但他在反托拉斯酒吧中很有影响力，正是他的想法构成了我们运作所处的功能失调的环境。
    对于本周针对Google提起的多起反托拉斯诉讼，很难不感到兴奋。上周三，得克萨斯州检察长肯·帕克斯顿（Ken Paxton）以及其他10家州立股份公司（AG）指责谷歌垄断在线广告，称谷歌使用强制性手段夺取了支撑所有广告资助互联网内容的管道的控制权，并非法分割了在线广告Facebook的广告市场。第二天，科罗拉多州的Phil Weiser和内布拉斯加州的道格·彼得森领导38个州，指控谷歌操纵其搜索结果以不利于Yelp等专业竞争对手，并阻止试图进入语音搜索或互联网支持的新搜索市场的竞争对手汽车。
  德克萨斯州（Dexas）案揭露了在线广告市场如何运作的新细节，这一点源于狄娜·斯里尼瓦桑（Dina Srinivasan）对广告销售如何转变为由Google经营的复杂金融市场的批判性研究。尽管该投诉指控Google进行了垄断，但它也指控另一种违反行为，即Facebook和Google陷入了卡特尔的行列，侵犯了用户隐私并在广告市场上定价。投诉显示，在Facebook购买了对用户（和FTC）承诺严格的隐私控制的WhatsApp之后，“ Facebook与谷歌签署了独家协议，授予谷歌访问数百万美国人的端到端加密的WhatsApp消息，照片的权限。 ，视频和音频文件。”如果属实，那将是Google和Facebook一系列明显的违法行为，并且背叛了他们的用户。
 申诉还断言，谷歌将广告市场与Facebook分开，如果Facebook退出了其他市场的直接竞争，它将为Facebook提供通过Google服务买卖广告的优势。从法律角度来看，这种勾结是有意义的。 《谢尔曼法》分为两个部分。第二部分禁止垄断，但是垄断案例很难提出，而且成本很高，并且需要精心设计的模型。第一节禁止将卡特尔和固定价格作为阴谋。卡特尔案子要容易得多-只需表明达成合作以定价的协议，就可以完成。实际上，卡特尔的起诉要容易得多，以至于价格固定是执法者实际提出刑事指控的唯一领域。对于Google来说，令人担忧的是，德克萨斯州指控卡特尔行为。
 科罗拉多州领导的西装虽然没有发掘任何惊天动地的东西，但也很有用。执法人员不仅要解决当今在台式机和移动平台上的搜索市场，而且要在未来搜索的方向发展，诸如支持互联网的汽车和语音助手之类的平台。这是确保反托拉斯执法在建立新市场（这是最容易产生竞争的时候）时阻止垄断的明智方法。 
自10月以来，执法人员对世界上最大的两家公司Google和Facebook提出了四项强力起诉。对于这些民事侵权行为，要求采取的补救措施很艰难。执法人员正在要求禁令救济以制止不良行为，这些公司的分拆以结束结构性冲突以及金钱损失和民事罚款。这几个月来可能是自二战以来最艰难的反托拉斯行动二战时期，哈里·杜鲁门（Harry Truman）在战争期间停职后重新启动了反托拉斯案件。
 不只是这些西装苹果正受到史诗游戏公司（Epic Games）和一个广泛的联盟的重大攻击，该联盟试图摧毁其应用商店的垄断地位，而国会正在加紧努力，通过立法努力粉碎垄断地位。正如史蒂文·佩尔斯坦（Steven Perlstein）在《华盛顿邮报》上指出的那样，这不仅是对Google和Facebook的攻击，而且是“对其他高度集中行业（制药，电信，金融服务）中的主导公司的合法射击”请注意，他们的不间断收购和严格的商业惯例可能会带来类似的挑战。”
 甚至欧洲人也变得更加积极进取，欧洲议会议员保罗·唐（Paul Tang）赢得了在欧盟禁止个人化广告的投票，并且对欧盟竞争执法者玛格丽特·韦斯特格（Margarethe Vestager）提出的批评越来越落后，因为后者落后于美国的强硬做法。
 在没有意识到垄断问题上政策界已经非常正确的情况下，很难不看到竞争政策的急剧转变。
 但是，随着我们从对主导公司的反托拉斯的理论构想转向实际实施，美中不足的是。就是说，西装要花很长时间。
  正在审理10月份提起诉讼的法官阿米特·梅塔（Amit Mehta）说，司法部和谷歌很可能会在2023年下半年进行审判。距现在将近三年。延迟的原因是有道理的；双方都必须收集文件，进行研究，归档和辩论程序动议，采访执行人员和利益相关者，并为审判建立复杂的经济模型。不过，三年就是三年，那是最少的。审判本身可能会进行延长并进入补救阶段，然后才会有上诉。一切都说完之后，可能需要五年或更长时间才能找到补救措施。
 如此长的时间的一个问题是，垄断行为持续的时间越长，在这种情况下，对广告收入被盗的发布商以及财产被盗用的中小型企业的损害就越大。我们可以量化一些额外的损失。 Google今年的收入为$ 170B，并且平均每年以10-20％的速度增长。如果我们降低10％的下限，到2025年Google的收入将再增加1000亿美元。这笔钱不菲。 Facebook今年的收入为800亿美元，但增长速度更快，因此收入的净增幅大致相同。换句话说，如果政府的主张是可信的，那么冗长的案子虽然可能是必要的，但也使这些垄断者每个人可以窃取额外的1000亿美元。 
垄断不仅是非法的，而且实际上是犯罪，是占统治地位的行为者侵占他人权利和财产的行为。漫长的审判从本质上讲类似于说银行抢劫犯会不断抢劫银行，直到他们被定罪为止，而且很可能会继续抢劫。法官可以通过各种方式发出初步命令，以制止暂时的不良行为，因此这些公司不可避免地要继续做下去。但是，法官们不喜欢发布这样的命令，尽管希望执行者会要求他们发出命令，而梅塔将会例外。
 但是货币成本并不是延误中最危险的部分。更可怕的是Google和Facebook助长了政治腐败。在过去的十年中，成千上万的报纸瓦解了，在接下来的三年中，成千上万的报纸将崩溃。除了杀死像报纸这样的亲社会机构之外，这些平台还一直在造成全社会的重大伤害，从造成国外种族清洗，西方民主国家分裂的局面到破坏我们的经济命脉。最终状态令人恐惧。确实，这是德州投诉所指的是Google的长期目标。
 Google当前的统治地位只是其未来计划的预览。 Google渴望完全控制市场，其最新目标是改变互联网的自由开放架构。 Google的计划是在互联网周围建立一个围墙花园，在其中控制网站和移动应用程序。 Google将其新兴企业称为[已编辑]，在这个世界中，发布商内容由Google经营...
 Google记录在案的计划是在开放的互联网上吸引在线发布者，并将其转变为内容创建者，例如YouTube内容创建者，从而在完全封闭的平台上为Google创收。
 Google拥有YouTube创作者的全部权力，可以将其取消货币化，审查或推广他们的能力。这是Google面向所有在线演讲者和企业的目标，使我们所有人都可以成为农奴，他们可以在Google的陪同下工作和演讲。很难争辩说，等待五年的补救措施就足以应对对我们的钱包以及更重要的是我们的自由造成的这一令人难以置信的威胁。
 现在，这并不是说这些案件在结案之前不会生效。我已经指出，DOJ诉讼已经产生了影响，因为Google现在正面临潜在的竞争，并且正在改变其行为以制止最恶劣的排他行为。随着围绕Google和Facebook的丑闻不断出现，这些公司减轻其行为的政治需求将会增加。
 法律学者蒂姆·吴（Tim Wu）将此现象称为“肘”反托拉斯执法理论的警察，并且有很多先例。在1990年代和2000年代初，在针对微软的反托拉斯案期间和之后，微软高管在产品开发方面变得更加谨慎，他们寻求法律建议以确保他们不会以反竞争的方式行事。比尔·盖茨（Bill Gates）最近指出，人们使用iPhone和Android手机的原因是，微软对案件分心，因此在使Windows Mobile成为标准方面迷失了方向。 IBM也有类似的故事，由于反托拉斯案，IBM在1960年代取消了对软件的捆绑销售，从而推动了现代软件行业的创建。 
由于手肘处的警察注视着他们的一举一动，因此Google和Facebook必须将法律建议纳入其产品开发模式，这样做可能会增加竞争对手的机会。
 尽管如此，2020年与早期相比还是有区别的。比尔·盖茨（Bill Gates）很难相信，他比扎克伯格（Zuckerberg）和皮查伊（Pichai）更遵守法律，因为数十年前法治更加强大。鉴于反托拉斯法的法律不确定性以及这些领导人的政治蔑视，马克·扎克伯格（Mark Zuckerberg）和桑达尔·皮查伊（Sundar Pichai）可能会非常努力地努力反腐败执法。他们将竭尽所能*不*改变不良行为，无论是确保产品继续排斥其他产品，通过更多收购继续进行合并收购（如Facebook所做的那样），甚至尝试使用其平台操纵选举（如优步（Uber）和Lyft（Lyft）与加利福尼亚的22号提案达成了协议。我们正处于精英无法无天的时代，因此，在强大的领导者开始担心被抓到触犯法律之前，反托拉斯的正常作用可能不会起作用。
 这就是为什么我需要开始谈论犯罪和惩罚的原因。
  在1930年代末期，我们经历了类似的精英违法时期，这是一场以新政形式投票支持政治变革的公众投票权的合法性的斗争。在1936年的选举中，银行，报纸和大型企业领导人通过各种资金充裕的利益集团联合起来，试图制止FDR的连任。他们失败。两年后，在经济衰退期间，这些领导人再次试图撤销新政规则，因为这违宪夺取了政权。
 进入美国历史上最伟大的反托拉斯执法机构瑟曼·阿诺德（Thurman Arnold）。阿诺德（Arnold）是怀俄明州的民主党人，但他是个偶像破坏者，既是新政的热心人士，又是好朋友，并且与芝加哥大学的反垄断保守派亨利·西蒙斯（Henry Simons）合作，后来帮助创立了芝加哥学派的法律和经济学运动。阿诺德（Arnold）是新闻界的追捧者，他认为公众​​需要通过他在新闻界解释的案件来理解反托拉斯法。他通过法院和新闻界主持了大规模的美铝垄断诉讼，在第二次世界大战期间，他将在国会作证，并指责新泽西州标准石油公司（Standard Oil of New Jersey）等巨头与纳粹分子（他们正在这样做）合作。
 1938年，阿诺德（Arnold）成为司法部反托拉斯部门的负责人。他采取了一种特殊的方法，将法律和秩序带回了商业界，这是将反托拉斯从仅由律师处理的商业问题转变为高管的重要社会污名。他实际上会起诉高管自己，将他们识别为普通罪犯。阿诺德认为，“使商人行事的唯一方法就是起诉的威胁。”瞧，当他这样做时，突然间价格降低了，反竞争行为也停止了。
 阿诺德还相信反托拉斯法刑事起诉的政治目的。他认为，政治民主取决于当时被称为“工业民主”的东西，这意味着开放的竞争性市场可以让普通民众参与。垄断和卡特尔化阻止了这种情况，因此应通过民事或刑事诉讼加以阻止，以确保业务规则保持公平和安全。毕竟，阿诺德（Arnold）问道，反托拉斯代表着“商业交易的红灯，如果交通违法是刑事犯罪，为什么我们不应该寻求刑事起诉呢？” 
阿诺德（Arnold）奉行的策略非常有效。他会＆＃34;重击，重击所有人，然后立即将其全部击中。”同时将刑事起诉和民事指控同时带给参与反竞争做法的各个层面的业内人士，然后提出同意令重组产业。商人实际上会在他的办公室外面排队，然后在他面前签字，然后签字，然后他的方法最终改变了“建筑和建筑，电影，轮胎，化肥，石油和运输业”等。通过追随大企业的不良行为，阿诺德成为该国最受欢迎的新经销商之一。
 然而，尽管阿诺德经常被誉为伟大的反托拉斯执法者，但他的真正遗产比这重要得多。他与其他新经销商一起，帮助结束了商业和银行业精英之间的犯罪热潮。今天，我们面临着类似的犯罪热潮。正如吉利德·埃德尔曼（Gilead Edelman）在Facebook和Google之间达成的分割广告市场的交易中在Wired上写道的那样：“如果德克萨斯州的指控属实，那么两家公司可能都违反了联邦反托拉斯法，并在此过程中犯了重罪。”
  到了基本点。 Facebook和Google所做的是犯罪，因此必须将其视为犯罪。目前，马克·扎克伯格和桑达尔·皮查伊认为，他们必须对民事案件进行抗辩，这意味着花钱买律师，并被政府官员罢免。但是他们会保留自己的财富，当然还有他们的自由，因为他们个人没有受到威胁，因此即使在公司受审的情况下，谋求在其他地方占领更多市场也是一个完全合理的选择。冒着他人生计的风险违反法律，最坏的情况是停车罚单。
 但是，如果这些人面临着个人刑事责任的前景，那么利益将突然转移。在审判期间，他们将变得更加谨慎，不愿采取可能的掠夺性行动，因为他们害怕失去自己的财富和自由，就像他们掠夺他人的财产和自由一样。当他们做出关键业务决策时，他们的律师将开始就法律上的合法性和不合法性向他们提供不同的建议。
 此外，使用刑法会产生不同的变化，并为执法者提供民事诉讼所没有的调查工具。司法部反托拉斯部制定了一项称为“公司宽恕政策”的政策，该协议使卡特尔参与者如果向该部门通报串谋，就可以宽大处理，从而激励不法行为者向司法部告知正在实施的犯罪。如果执法人员开始对大型高科技企业高管进行真正的刑事起诉，使其垄断，那么他们将能够以现在无法做到的方式翻转高管。想象一下，有无数的Facebook或Google员工，意识到企图垄断，突然害怕害怕进监狱，如果他们不站出来告诉执法人员他们知道什么。我怀疑扎克伯格或皮查伊对员工的任何问题都会被大幅放大，如果员工担心他们可能会因为共同犯罪者而被指控犯罪。
 执法人员可以使用《谢尔曼反托拉斯法》提起刑事诉讼，因为该法律既是刑事法规又是民事法规。在1980年代之前的某个时候，执法人员停止提出刑事条文以解决垄断问题，但他们的确对卡特尔之间的价格操纵提出了刑事指控。反托拉斯部门最近起诉了养鸡公司的高管，尽管他们基本上认为垄断是纯粹的民事错误。我认为，这是错误的，因为《谢尔曼法》的犯罪方面还有其他闲置的法律工具。的确，有史以来最大的一些案件，包括刑事垄断指控，例如1940年代针对A＆amp; P连锁超市的案件（当时是沃尔玛或亚马逊）。
 尽管我认为突破法律界限是有用的，但没有必要开创性地利用《谢尔曼法案》的垄断条款对大型高科技企业高管提起刑事诉讼。固定价格本身已经是非法的，司法部反托拉斯部经常起诉人们从事这种卡特尔行为，而固定价格是德克萨斯州指责Google和Facebook这样做的一件事。但是您甚至不必使用反托拉斯法，因为定期发生大量且例行的违法行为。例如，去年，Facebook因指控其夸大了视频观看指标而支付了4000万美元，这是另一种说法，称它很可能对广告商进行欺诈。但是，还有许多其他领域可能会做错事情。 
还有大量调查正在进行中，还有可能采取行动。例如，上周，联邦贸易委员会从九个社交媒体和视频流媒体服务发出了一些引起注意的信息请求：亚马逊，TikTok，Discord，Facebook，Reddit，Snap，Twitter，WhatsApp和YouTube。其中一个部分是要求提供有关虚假帐户，广告客户指标不准确，错误的收入价值以及高层领导参与监督的信息。谁知道会发掘出什么东西？
 最重要的是，刑事执法实际上对于纠正我们在商业领袖中看到的违法行为至关重要，因为我们既需要非掠夺性业务的安全环境，又需要有关于法治本身的强有力的声明。垄断是犯罪。是时候开始这样对待它了。
  即将结束的私募股权投资：我已经注意到，大型杠杆收购是傻瓜的黄金，并且投资者逐渐认识到，将资金投入大型私募股权基金并不会获得太多收益。对于体育巨人来说，这是一个问题，因为他们需要退休金。

Welcome to BIG, a newsletter about the politics of monopoly and finance. If you’d like to sign up, you can do so  here. Or just read on…
 Today’s issue is about the various antitrust cases against Google and Facebook announced this week, and why it’s time to issue criminal indictments against the executives responsible for the bad behavior. Plus:
  First, some house-keeping. Author and gadfly to the elites Anand Giridharadas  interviewed me about the Facebook case. Also, antitrust lawyer Shaoul Sussman and I wrote  a piece on a centrist scholar and historian named Herb Hovenkamp. Hovenkamp is not well-known outside the antitrust bar, but he is enormously influential within it, and it is his ideas that are structuring the dysfunctional environment in which we are operating.
    It’s hard not to be excited about the multiple antitrust suits filed this week against Google. This past Wednesday, Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton, along with 10 other state AGs,  accused Google of monopolizing online advertising, arguing it used coercive tactics to seize control of the plumbing that underpins all ad-financed internet content, and illegally divided up the online ad market with Facebook. The next day, Colorado’s Phil Weiser and Nebraska’s Doug Peterson led 38 states in  accusing Google of manipulating its search results to disfavor specialized competitors like Yelp, as well as blocking competitors who seek to enter new search markets like those of voice assistants or internet-enabled cars.
  The Texas case reveals new details about how online advertising markets function, drawing from Dina Srinivasan’s critical  research on how advertising sales has been transformed into a complex financial market run by Google. While the complaint alleges that Google has engaged in monopolization, it also alleges a different violation, that Facebook and Google are in a cartel to violate user privacy and fix prices in advertising markets. The complaint reveals that after Facebook bought WhatsApp, which pledged to its users (and the FTC) strict privacy controls, “Facebook signed an exclusive agreement with Google, granting Google access to millions of Americans’ end-to-end encrypted WhatsApp messages, photos, videos, and audio files.” If true, that’s a remarkable set of illegal acts, by both Google and Facebook, as well as a betrayal of their users.
 The complaint also asserts Google divided the ad market with Facebook, offering Facebook advantages in buying and selling ads through Google services if Facebook withdrew from head-to-head competition in other markets. This collusion is meaningful from a legal perspective. The Sherman Act has two parts. Section Two prohibits monopolization, but monopolization cases are very hard to bring and quite expensive, and require elaborate models. Section One prohibits cartels and price-fixing as conspiracies. Cartel cases are much easier - just show an agreement to collaborate on fixing prices, and you’re done. In fact cartels are so much easier to prosecute that price-fixing is the only area that enforcers actually bring criminal charges. And worrisome for Google, Texas is alleging cartel behavior.
 The Colorado-led suit, while not unearthing anything earth-shattering, is also quite useful. Enforcers there are addressing not just today’s search markets on desktop and mobile platforms, but where search is heading in the future, platforms like internet-enabled cars and voice assistants. It’s a smart way to ensure that antitrust enforcement blocks monopolization at the creation of new markets, which is when it’s easiest to generate competition.
 Since October, enforcers have brought four strong suits against Google and Facebook, two of the largest corporations in the world. And the demanded remedies for these civil violations are tough. Enforcers are asking for injunctive relief to stop the bad behavior, break-ups of these companies to end the structural conflicts, as well as monetary damages and civil fines.These few months represent perhaps the toughest spate of antitrust action since the post-World War II era, when Harry Truman restarted antitrust cases after their suspension during the war.
 It’s not just these suits; Apple is facing a  major attack from Epic Games and a broad coalition who seeks to destroy its app store monopoly, and Congress is  gearing up to smash monopolies through legislative efforts. As Steven Perlstein  noted in the Washington Post, this effort is more than just an attack on Google and Facebook, but a “legal shot across the bow of dominant firms in other highly concentrated industries — pharmaceuticals, telecommunications, financial services — who are now on notice that their nonstop acquisitions and hardball business practices could invite similar challenge.”
 Even Europeans are getting more aggressive, with European member of Parliament Paul Tang  winning a vote to ban personalized advertising in the EU, as well as offering increasing  criticism of EU Competition enforcer Margarethe Vestager for lagging behind the tougher approach in the United States.
 It’s hard not to see the sharp turn in competition policy without a recognition that something has gone very very right in policy circles on the question of monopoly.
 And yet, there’s a fly in the ointment, as we move from the theoretical idea of antitrust against dominant corporations to the actual implementation. And that is, the suits are going to take a very long time.
  Judge Amit Mehta, who is hearing the case filed in October, said  that the DOJ and Google will likely go to trial in late 2023. That’s nearly three years from now. The reason for the delay makes sense; both sides must gather documents, do research, file and debate procedural motions, interview executives and stakeholders, and build complex economic models for the trial. Still, three years is three years, and that’s a minimum. The trial itself could stretch out, with a remedy phase, and then there will be appeals. When all is said and done, it could be five years before there’s a remedy, or even longer.
 One problem with such a lengthy period is that the longer monopolistic behavior goes on, the more damage, in this case to publishers whose ad revenue is being stolen, and small and medium size businesses whose property is being appropriated. We can quantify the additional damages, somewhat. Google has $170B in revenue this year, and is growing on average at 10-20% a year. If we take the lower end of 10%, Google will add another $100 billion to its revenue by 2025. That’s a lot of money. Facebook is at $80 billion of revenue this year, but it is growing faster, so the net increase of revenue is a roughly similar amount. In other words, if the claims of the government are credible, then the lengthy case, while perhaps necessary, is also enabling these monopolists to steal an additional $100 billion apiece.
 Monopolization isn’t just illegal, it is in fact a crime, an appropriation of the rights and property of others by a dominant actor. The lengthy trial is essentially akin to saying that bank robbers getting to keep robbing banks until they are convicted, and can probably keep the additional loot. There are ways of a judge issuing preliminary orders to stop bad behavior in the interim, so it’s not inevitable that these corporations get to continue what they are doing. However, judges don’t tend to like issuing such orders, though hopefully enforcers will ask for them and Mehta will make an exception.
 But the monetary cost is not the most dangerous part of the delay. What’s more frightening is the political corruption that Google and Facebook are enabling. Thousands of newspapers have fallen apart over the past ten years, and over the next three, thousands more will collapse. Aside from killing pro-social institutions like newspapers, these platforms have been inducing significant harms society-wide, from enabling  ethnic cleansing abroad and divisiveness in Western democracies, to  undermining our economy writ large. The end state is frightening. Indeed, here’s what the Texas complaint alleges is Google’s long-term goal.
 Google’s current dominance is merely a preview of its future plans. Google has an appetite for total dominance, and its latest ambition is to transform the free and open architecture of the internet. Google’s plan is to create a walled garden around the internet in which it controls websites and mobile applications. Google calls its emerging venture the [redacted], a world in which publisher content is operated by Google…
 Google’s documented plan is to capture online publishers on the open internet and transform them into content creators generating revenue for Google on a completely closed platform—like YouTube content creators.
 Google has total power over YouTube creators, the ability to demonetize them, to censor them, promote them or not. And that’s Google’s goal for all speakers and businesses online, to turn us all into serfs working - and speaking - at Google’s pleasure. It’s hard to argue that waiting five years for a remedy is sufficient to address this incredible threat to our wallets and more importantly our liberties.
 Now, this is not to say these cases won’t have an effect until they are concluded. I’ve pointed out that the DOJ suit is  already having an effect, in that Google is now  facing potential competition and changing its behavior to stop the most egregious exclusionary behavior. As scandal after scandal emerges around Google and Facebook, the political need for these companies to mitigate their behavior will increase.
 Legal scholar Tim Wu calls such a phenomenon  the Policeman at the Elbow theory of antitrust enforcement, and there’s a lot of precedent. In the 1990s and early 2000s, during and after the antitrust case against Microsoft, Microsoft executives became far more cautious in product development, seeking legal advice to ensure they were not behaving in anti-competitive ways. Bill Gates  recently noted that the reason people use iPhones and Android phones is because Microsoft was distracted by the case and so lost out on making Windows Mobile the standard. There’s a similar story with IBM, which unbundled software in the 1960s as a result of an antitrust case, thus enabling the creation of the modern software industry.
 Because of the policeman at the elbow watching their every move, Google and Facebook are going to have to incorporate legal advice into their product development patterns, and doing so is likely to increase opportunities for competitors.
 Still, there’s a difference between 2020 and earlier periods. Bill Gates, as hard as it is to believe, was more law-abiding than Zuckerberg and Pichai, because the rule of law was much stronger decades ago. Given legal uncertainty around the point of antitrust laws, as well as the political defiance of these leaders, it’s likely that Mark Zuckerberg and Sundar Pichai are going to work extremely hard to corrupt antitrust enforcement. They will do everything they can to *not* change their bad behavior, whether that’s ensuring products continue to exclude others, continuing a merger spree with more acquisitions (as Facebook  just did), or even attempting to use their platforms to manipulate elections (as Uber and Lyft did with Prop 22 in California). We are in an era of elite lawlessness, so the normal effects of antitrust may not work until powerful leaders start to be afraid of getting caught breaking the law.
 And that’s what brings me to why we need to start talking about crime and punishment.
  In the late 1930s, we had a similar period of elite lawlessness, a battle over the very legitimacy of the public’s right to vote for political change in the form of the New Deal. In the election of 1936, the banks, newspapers, and big business leaders combined through a variety of well-funded interest groups in an attempt to thwart FDR’s reelection. They lost. Two years later, in the midst of a recession, these leaders sought again to roll back New Deal rules as an unconstitutional seizures of power.
 Enter Thurman Arnold, the greatest antitrust enforcer in American history. Arnold was a Wyoming Democrat, but he was an iconoclast, both an ardent New Dealer and good friends and collaborator with Henry Simons, an anti-monopolist conservative at the University of Chicago who later helped found the Chicago School law and economics movement. Arnold was a press hound, believing that the public needed to understand antitrust law through the cases he would explain in the press. He shepherded the massive Alcoa monopoly suit through the courts and the press, and during World War II he would testify before Congress and accuse Standard Oil of New Jersey, among other giants, of collaborating with Nazis (which they were doing).
 In 1938, Arnold became the head of the antitrust division at the Department of Justice. He took a specific approach to bringing law and order back to the business community, which was to turn antitrust from a mere business problem to be handled by lawyers into a significant social stigma for executives. He would actually indict the executives themselves, fingerprinting them as common criminals. Arnold believed  that “the only thing that would make businessmen behave was the threat of indictment.” And lo and behold, when he did so, all of a sudden there were lower prices in the and anti-competitive practices stopped.
 Arnold also believed in the political purpose of criminal prosecution for antitrust law. Political democracy, he believed, depended on what was known at the time as “industrial democracy,” meaning open competitive markets in which ordinary people could participate. Monopolization and cartelization prevented that, and thus should be blocked through either civil or criminal proceedings to ensure the rules of business preserved fairness and safety. After all, Arnold asked, antitrust represented the “red lights of business traffic, and why shouldn’t we seek criminal indictments if traffic violations are criminal offenses?”
 The strategy Arnold pursued worked remarkably well. He would "hit hard, hit everyone and hit them all at once,” bringing both criminal indictments and civil charges concurrently to everyone in the industry at all layers who were involved in anti-competitive practices, and then offering a consent decree to restructure the industry. Businessmen would practically line up outside his office, and sign whatever he put in front of them, and his approach ended up changing the “building and construction, motion picture, tire, fertilizer, petroleum, and transportation industries,” among others. By going after bad behavior by big business, Arnold became one of the most popular New Dealers in the country.
 Yet while Arnold is often lauded as a great antitrust enforcer, his true legacy is much more important than that. Along with other New Dealers, he helped end the crime spree that had taken place among business and banking elites. Today, we face a similar crime spree. As Gilead Edelman wrote  in Wired on the deal between Facebook and Google to divide up the ad market, “If what Texas is alleging is true, then both companies may have violated federal antitrust law—and committed felonies in the process.”
  And that gets to the basic point. What Facebook and Google are doing is crime, and it needs to be treated as such. Right now, Mark Zuckerberg and Sundar Pichai believe that they have to fight a civil case, which means spending money on lawyers and being deposed by government officials. But they will keep their wealth, and of course, their freedom, because they are personally not at risk, so scheming to dominate more markets elsewhere is a perfectly reasonable activity to pursue even while their companies are on trial. Violating the law by taking someone else’s livelihood risks, at worst, a parking ticket.
 But if these men were facing the prospect of personal criminal liability, then the stakes would suddenly shift. During the trial, they would become far more cautious and unwilling to engage in potentially predatory actions, for fear of losing their wealth and freedom, much as they have appropriated that of others. And their lawyers would start giving them different advice on what is legal and what is not when they come to key business decisions.
 Moreover, using criminal laws creates a different dynamic and gives enforcers investigative tools that don’t exist for civil suits. The DOJ Antitrust Division has a policy called the “Corporate Leniency Policy,” which lets cartel participants get leniency if they tell the division about the conspiracy, thus creating an incentive for wrongdoers to tell the DOJ about crimes in action. If enforcers began to bring real criminal indictments against big tech executives for monopolization, they would be able to flip executives in ways they cannot now do. Imagine any number of Facebook or Google employees, aware of attempts to monopolize and suddenly afraid of being put in jail if they don’t come forward and tell enforcers what they know. I suspect that any problems Zuckerberg or Pichai have with employees would be magnified dramatically if employees feared they might face charges as co-conspirators in a criminal act.
 Enforcers can use the Sherman Antitrust Act to bring criminal charges, as the law is both a criminal and a civil statute. At some point prior to the 1980s, enforcers stopped bringing criminal provisions to address monopolization, but they do bring criminal charges for price-fixing among cartels. The Antitrust Division recently  indicted chicken company execs for price-fixing, though they basically consider monopolization a purely civil wrong. In my view, that is a mistake, with the criminal aspect of the Sherman Act yet one more dormant legal tool lying fallow. Indeed, some of the biggest cases ever brought, like the case against the A&P supermarket chain in the 1940s (which was the Walmart or Amazon of its day), included criminal monopolization charges.
 Though I think pushing the boundaries of the law is useful, it is not necessary to pioneer a new use of the Sherman Act’s monopolization provisions to bring criminal charges against big tech executives. Price-fixing is already per se illegal, and the DOJ Antitrust Division often does indict people for engaging in this kind of cartel behavior, and price-fixing is one thing Texas accused Google and Facebook of doing. But you don’t even have to use the antitrust laws, because there are ample and routine violations of law happening on a regular basis. Last year, for instance, Facebook paid  $40 million over accusations it inflated video viewing metrics, which is another way of saying it likely committed fraud against advertisers. But there are many other areas of likely wrongdoing to explore.
 There are also plenty of investigations going on, and potential fodder for action. Last week, for instance, the Federal Trade Commission issued a little  noticed request for information from nine social media and video streaming services: Amazon, TikTok, Discord, Facebook, Reddit, Snap, Twitter, WhatsApp, and YouTube. One of the sections was a request for information about false accounts, inaccuracies in metrics for advertisers, revenue value of errors, and senior leadership involvement in oversight. Who knows what that will unearth?
 The bottom line is that criminal enforcement is in fact critical to redressing the lawlessness we’re seeing among business leaders, because we need both a safe environment for non-predatory business, and a strong statement about the rule of law itself. Monopolization is a crime. It’s time to start treating it as such.
  The End of Private Equity Is Coming: I’ve noted that  large leveraged buyouts are fool’s gold, and that investors are coming to understand that they aren’t getting a great deal when they put their money into large PE funds. That’s a problem for PE titans because they need pension fund money.
